page: Google Cloud Platform c4d-standard-96-lssd is a server instance built on the x86_64 CPU architecture. It is configured with 96 dedicated vCPUs and 372 GB of system memory, providing an allocation of approximately 3.88 GB of RAM per core. The instance does not include GPU accelerators, and the local storage size is designated as None in the system specifications.

Operating with dedicated CPU allocation, this server type ensures consistent compute resources without the virtualization overhead or resource contention typical of shared-core configurations. The 1:4 ratio of vCPUs to memory aligns with general-purpose resource distribution, balancing compute capacity with memory availability. No benchmark data is available for this specific instance type to evaluate raw execution speeds or memory bandwidth.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the high density of 96 dedicated cores makes this instance suitable for large-scale parallel processing workloads that require substantial CPU capacity without massive memory-to-core ratios. Appropriate workloads include web serving, general-purpose enterprise applications, and distributed compute tasks that do not require local SSD storage or GPU acceleration.
description: Google Cloud Platform c4d-standard-96-lssd is an x86_64 architecture server configuration equipped with 96 dedicated vCPUs and 372 GB of system memory. This resource allocation provides approximately 3.88 GB of RAM per core, establishing a balanced general-purpose profile. The instance features dedicated CPU allocation to prevent resource contention, though it lacks GPU accelerators and local storage. Qualitatively, the high core count offers significant compute density for parallel processing tasks. Without benchmark data to measure specific performance metrics, the hardware configuration indicates suitability for multi-threaded enterprise applications, web serving, and general-purpose distributed workloads requiring high CPU capacity and standard memory ratios. This configuration is designed for workloads that benefit from dedicated physical resources without the need for specialized hardware accelerators.
